title: Razumovia lepidota
intro: Razumovia lepidota is a plant species native to the subtropical regions of Central America, commonly known as 'Yucatecan tree daisy' or 'Razumovia'. The plant has been traditionally used for its medicinal properties, offering potential health benefits such as anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial effects, which can aid in the treatment of various conditions, including wounds and skin irritations. The bioactive constituents of Razumovia lepidota include sesquiterpene lactones, flavonoids, and phenolic compounds, which are believed to contribute to its medicinal properties. The plant is commonly used in herbal preparations, including teas, tinctures, and infusions, which are used to treat a range of health issues, including fever, respiratory problems, and digestive issues.
taxonomy: Razumovia lepidota is classified within the family Asteraceae, commonly known as the daisy or sunflower family. This classification places it among the flowering plants, specifically within the order Asterales. Further, it belongs to the family's subfamily Barnadesioideae, which comprises several genera of flowering plants. Within the Barnadesioideae subfamily, Razumovia lepidota is placed in the tribe Barnadesieae. Its specific classification within the family Asteraceae provides a sense of its evolutionary relationships and biological characteristics.
